item.title: Who pays for AI?
item.description: And how much?

item.title: A Europe that’s losing at tech could still achieve quantum supremacy
item.description: The UK and EU have committed twice as much money as the US to investments in the computing sector

item.title: Why Iran is returning to war
item.description: Its rulers believe that further escalation will force the US to offer the security and economic relief it craves

item.title: The dangers of investor fatalism
item.description: Reports of a Russo-Chinese plan to disable satellites underline how markets are ignoring geopolitical threats to the tech boom

item.title: Writers with an itch for unseen nature
item.description: A growing body of books asks us to look beyond big animals to the fascinations of the small and the hidden

item.title: The lessons Burnham must heed on the economy
item.description: Policy must go beyond mere announcements and budget arithmetic

item.title: Would the corgis love the new Queen Elizabeth II Garden in Regent’s Park?
item.description: Drought-resistant, naturalistic planting makes this site unlike any other Royal Park — and all the better for it

item.title: The great British corporate sell-off
item.description: The UK’s business case needs strengthening to stop a self-reinforcing exodus

item.title: Uber’s super app ‘synergies’ deserve some suspension of disbelief
item.description: Ride-hailing giant’s purchase of Delivery Hero may be more appetising than it looks

item.title: We need humanities more than ever in the age of AI
item.description: Subjects like literature, languages and history are vital to a thoughtful and prosperous society

item.title: Why socialists should embrace luxury apartments
item.description: High-end developments can be more effective at unlocking affordability than social housing programmes

item.title: Perps are distracting Wall Street, not disrupting it
item.description: Craze for perpetual futures is worrying investors in securities exchanges

item.title: Vibes, not results, will dictate Burnham’s true election timetable
item.description: The political realities point to the new PM going long — but not too long

item.title: The digital euro gets ready for beta testing
item.description: A brave new world for EU digital finance

item.title: The joy of watching geniuses deliver
item.description: Individual stars shone brighter than ever at this World Cup

item.title: ESG is dead
item.description: Long live ESG

item.title: Conflicts of interest are back — and more blatant than ever
item.description: Democrats would do well to focus their fire on the use of public office by Donald Trump’s circle for private gain

item.title: The perks of parenting by spreadsheet
item.description: One simple trick to share it equally

item.title: Takanomics vs Abenomics: the $2.3tn prizefight
item.description: Japan’s new prime minister has broken with her mentor by encouraging firms to invest according to a government plan

item.title: The rating reforms needed to tackle private credit’s vulnerabilities
item.description: Once again rating agencies are the centre of systemic concerns

item.title: Renewed Hormuz tensions raise chances of a pricey winter
item.description: Asia and Europe would end up competing for LNG cargoes during their cold season if market is short, pushing up prices

item.title: IBM’s profit warning shows tech valuations are all in the timing
item.description: There is a case for believing AI revenue will grow more slowly than the hyperscalers have baked into their plans

item.title: Ukraine’s self-defeating reshuffle
item.description: Sacking of the defence minister is a setback for the country’s military efforts

item.title: PayPal investors can afford not to check out too hastily
item.description: Advent International and Stripe’s $53bn offer is a bargain for the buyers

item.title: The UK finally has a blueprint for innovation — now it must act on it
item.description: The Whittle Lab aims to transform aerothermal engineering, one of the country’s strongest manufacturing industries
